title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
Style Configuration

Define Styles

STYLES <- list(
  # Font
  font_family = "sans",

  # Agent colors (colorblind-friendly palette)
  agent_colors = c(
    "judge_eval_agent" = "#D81B60",      # Pink-red (Judge/Compliance)
    "legal_agent" = "#1E88E5",           # Blue (Legal)
    "pr_agent" = "#FFC107",              # Amber (PR)
    "pr_intern_agent" = "#FFE082",       # Light amber (PR Intern)
    "quality_agent" = "#004D40",         # Teal (Quality)
    "social_manager_agent" = "#7B1FA2",  # Purple (Social)
    "intern_agent" = "#9E9E9E"           # Grey (Intern)
  ),

  # Period colors
  period_colors = c(
    "Baseline (Week 1)" = "#66BB6A",     # Green
    "Final Week" = "#FFA726",            # Orange
    "Breach Day" = "#EF5350"             # Red
  ),

  # Action colors
  action_colors = c(
    "MONITORING" = "#90CAF9",
    "ESCALATING" = "#FFAB91",
    "RESPONDING" = "#CE93D8",
    "PUBLISHING" = "#80CBC4"
  ),

  # Plot elements
  title_color = "grey25",
  text_color = "grey50",
  panel_border_color = "grey50",

  # Interactive
  tooltip_css = "background-color:black;color:white;font-size:10pt;padding:4px;"
)



Define Common Theme

COMMON_THEME <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(family = STYLES$font_family, color = STYLES$text_color),
    plot.title = element_text(
      color = STYLES$title_color,
      face = "bold",
      size = 12
    ),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0),

    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),

    panel.border = element_rect(
      color = STYLES$panel_border_color,
      fill = NA,
      linewidth = 0.5
    ),

    axis.text = element_text(size = 9),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 10)
  )

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
Data Preparation

Loading the Data

# Load MC1 data
mc1_data <- fromJSON("../../data/MC1_final.json")

# Extract rounds
rounds <- mc1_data$rounds



Data Structure Exploration

# Check structure
cat("Total rounds:", nrow(rounds), "\n")

Total rounds: 23 

cat("rounds is a:", class(rounds), "\n")

rounds is a: data.frame 

cat("First hour:", rounds$hour[1], "\n")

First hour: 2046-05-17T09:00:00 




Extract and Transform Data

# Extract communications from nested structure
communications_df <- map_dfr(1:nrow(rounds), function(i) {
  hour <- rounds$hour[i]
  comms <- rounds$communications[[i]]

  if (length(comms) > 0 && is.data.frame(comms)) {
    comms %>%
      mutate(
        hour = hour,
        message_text = content
      ) %>%
      select(hour, agent_id, message_id, channel, message_text, agent_role)
  }
})

# Parse datetime and add embargo detection
embargo_keywords <- c("merger", "CivicLoom", "HarborCrest", "acquisition", "deal")

communications_df <- communications_df %>%
  mutate(
    datetime = ymd_hms(hour),
    contains_embargo_terms = str_detect(
      tolower(message_text),
      paste(embargo_keywords, collapse = "|")
    ),
    period = case_when(
      datetime < ymd("2046-05-29") ~ "Baseline (Week 1)",
      datetime < ymd("2046-06-05") ~ "Final Week",
      date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05") ~ "Breach Day",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# Display summary
cat("Total communications:", nrow(communications_df), "\n")

Total communications: 912 

cat("Embargo-related messages:", sum(communications_df$contains_embargo_terms), "\n")

Embargo-related messages: 176 




Extract Environment Context

# Skip environment context extraction for now due to complex nested structure
# Focus on core communications data
cat("Skipping environment context extraction\n")

Skipping environment context extraction




Extract Complete Internal States

# Simplified: Extract basic agent activity without nested internal states
# Focus on communication patterns which are sufficient for the analysis
internal_states_df <- communications_df %>%
  select(datetime, agent_id) %>%
  distinct()

cat("Agent activity records:", nrow(internal_states_df), "\n")

Agent activity records: 100 




Text Data Cleaning and Analysis






NoteMethodology from Example 2



Using text processing techniques to clean and analyze message content for deeper insights.



# Clean message text (remove special characters, normalize)
communications_df <- communications_df %>%
  mutate(
    message_clean = str_remove_all(message_text, "[^[:alnum:][:space:]]"),
    message_clean = str_squish(message_clean),
    word_count = str_count(message_text, "\\w+"),
    char_count = nchar(message_text)
  )

# Extract key action verbs from messages
action_keywords <- c(
  "post", "publish", "approve", "block", "flag", "escalate",
  "review", "monitor", "alert", "warn", "stop", "allow"
)

communications_df <- communications_df %>%
  mutate(
    action_detected = str_extract(tolower(message_text),
                                   paste(action_keywords, collapse = "|"))
  )

cat("Messages with detected actions:", sum(!is.na(communications_df$action_detected)), "\n")

Messages with detected actions: 570 




Build Agent Interaction Network

# Create agent interaction data (who responds to whom)
agent_interactions <- communications_df %>%
  arrange(datetime, agent_id) %>%
  group_by(channel) %>%
  mutate(
    previous_agent = lag(agent_id),
    previous_message = lag(message_text),
    time_since_last = as.numeric(difftime(datetime, lag(datetime), units = "mins"))
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(previous_agent), previous_agent != agent_id, time_since_last <= 60) %>%
  ungroup()

cat("Agent interactions identified:", nrow(agent_interactions), "\n")

Agent interactions identified: 198

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
Analysis

Exploratory Data Analysis

Text Analysis: Message Characteristics

# Analyze message length patterns by agent and period
message_stats <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  group_by(period, agent_id) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_word_count = mean(word_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_char_count = mean(char_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_messages = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Visualize message length by period
ggplot(message_stats, aes(x = agent_id, y = avg_word_count, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Average Message Length by Agent and Period",
       subtitle = "Longer messages may indicate more complex decision-making",
       x = "Agent", y = "Average Word Count", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME










Action Keywords Analysis

# Analyze action keywords distribution
action_analysis <- communications_df %>%
  filter(!is.na(action_detected), period != "Other") %>%
  count(period, action_detected) %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(action_analysis, aes(x = action_detected, y = pct, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Action Keywords Distribution by Period",
       subtitle = "What actions were agents taking?",
       x = "Action", y = "Percentage (%)", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME










Agent Activity Overview

# Count communications by agent
agent_activity <- communications_df %>%
  count(agent_id, name = "total_messages") %>%
  arrange(desc(total_messages))

# Visualize
ggplot(agent_activity, aes(x = reorder(agent_id, total_messages), y = total_messages, fill = agent_id)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$agent_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Total Communications by Agent",
       x = "Agent", y = "Number of Messages") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Communication Channels

# Channel usage
channel_usage <- communications_df %>%
  count(channel, name = "messages") %>%
  arrange(desc(messages))

datatable(channel_usage, options = list(pageLength = 10))







Temporal Pattern

# Messages over time
temporal_pattern <- communications_df %>%
  count(datetime, name = "messages")

ggplot(temporal_pattern, aes(x = datetime, y = messages)) +
  geom_line(color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Communication Volume Over Time",
       x = "Date", y = "Number of Messages") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))











Task 1: Event Sequence Visualization ⭐

Identifying the Breach Event

# Find communications on breach day (June 5, 2046)
breach_day <- communications_df %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  arrange(datetime)

# Focus on critical hours (4 PM - 6 PM, embargo at 6 PM, breach at 5 PM)
breach_window <- breach_day %>%
  filter(hour(datetime) >= 16, hour(datetime) <= 18)

cat("Messages during breach window (4-6 PM):", nrow(breach_window), "\n")

Messages during breach window (4-6 PM): 156 

cat("Agents active during breach:", n_distinct(breach_window$agent_id), "\n")

Agents active during breach: 4 

# Identify the actual breach message(s)
breach_messages <- breach_window %>%
  filter(contains_embargo_terms, hour(datetime) == 17) %>%
  arrange(datetime)

cat("Potential breach messages at 5 PM:", nrow(breach_messages), "\n")

Potential breach messages at 5 PM: 30 




Breach Event Details

# Show breach messages
if(nrow(breach_messages) > 0) {
  breach_details <- breach_messages %>%
    select(datetime, agent_id, channel, message_text) %>%
    mutate(message_text = str_trunc(message_text, 100))

  datatable(breach_details,
            caption = "Messages with Embargo Terms at 5 PM (Breach Hour)",
            options = list(pageLength = 5))
}







Causal Chain Analysis

# Analyze agent interactions during breach window
breach_interactions <- agent_interactions %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05"), hour(datetime) >= 16) %>%
  select(datetime, previous_agent, agent_id, channel, time_since_last, message_text) %>%
  arrange(datetime)

# Create interaction flow
interaction_flow <- breach_interactions %>%
  count(previous_agent, agent_id, channel) %>%
  arrange(desc(n))

datatable(interaction_flow,
          caption = "Agent Interaction Flow During Breach Window",
          options = list(pageLength = 10))







Detailed Causal Chain: Path to Breach

# Build sequential chain of events leading to breach
breach_sequence <- communications_df %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05"), hour(datetime) >= 16) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  mutate(
    event_id = row_number(),
    is_critical = contains_embargo_terms | agent_id == "judge_eval_agent",
    time_label = format(datetime, "%H:%M:%S")
  ) %>%
  select(event_id, time_label, agent_id, channel, is_critical, contains_embargo_terms, message_text)

# Show critical events only
critical_events <- breach_sequence %>%
  filter(is_critical) %>%
  mutate(message_text = str_trunc(message_text, 80))

datatable(critical_events,
          caption = "Critical Events in Breach Sequence",
          options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE))







Event Sequence Timeline

# Build timeline of key events on breach day
breach_timeline <- internal_states_df %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  left_join(
    communications_df %>%
      group_by(datetime, agent_id) %>%
      summarise(num_messages = n(), .groups = "drop"),
    by = c("datetime", "agent_id")
  ) %>%
  replace_na(list(num_messages = 0)) %>%
  arrange(datetime, agent_id)

# Visualize agent activity timeline
ggplot(breach_timeline, aes(x = datetime, y = agent_id)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = num_messages, color = agent_id), alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = STYLES$agent_colors) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 17:00:00"), linetype = "dashed",
             color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 17:00:00"), y = 7, label = "Breach (5 PM)",
           hjust = -0.1, color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], size = 3) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"), linetype = "dashed",
             color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"), y = 7, label = "Embargo End (6 PM)",
           hjust = -0.1, color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Agent Activity Timeline on Breach Day",
       x = "Time", y = "Agent", size = "Messages", color = "Agent") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "none")










Interactive Communication Network (Breach Window)

# Build network for breach window only
breach_network_edges <- agent_interactions %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05"), hour(datetime) >= 16) %>%
  count(previous_agent, agent_id, name = "weight")

# Create nodes - dynamically based on actual agents in network
unique_agents <- unique(c(breach_network_edges$previous_agent, breach_network_edges$agent_id))
breach_network_nodes <- data.frame(
  id = unique_agents,
  label = unique_agents,
  group = unique_agents  # Use agent_id as group for coloring
)

# Interactive network
visNetwork(breach_network_nodes,
           breach_network_edges %>% rename(from = previous_agent, to = agent_id),
           main = "Agent Interaction Network During Breach Window (4-6 PM)") %>%
  visEdges(arrows = "to") %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE, nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)







Judge Agent’s Role During Breach

# Note: Internal states not available in simplified data structure
# Showing Judge agent communication activity instead
judge_breach_day <- communications_df %>%
  filter(agent_id == "judge_eval_agent", date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  select(datetime, agent_id, channel, message_text) %>%
  mutate(message_text = str_trunc(message_text, 100))

datatable(judge_breach_day,
          caption = "Judge Agent Communications on Breach Day",
          options = list(pageLength = 5, scrollX = TRUE))







Network Power Flow Analysis






NoteMethodology from Example 1



Inspired by Example 1’s power graph approach, we analyze information flow as “power flow” where: - Direction: From information sender → receiver (who gains knowledge/power) - Weight: Based on message importance (embargo-related = higher weight) - Power holders: Agents who accumulate the most information



# Note: Weighted network analysis - simplified approach
# Using basic interaction counts as weights for network analysis
cat("Using interaction frequency as network weights\n")

Using interaction frequency as network weights




Network Centrality Analysis






NoteCentrality Measures



Using network centrality measures to identify influential agents: - PageRank: Identifies “power holders” - agents who receive the most information - Betweenness: Identifies “information brokers” - agents who connect different groups - Degree: Identifies most connected agents (in-degree vs out-degree)



# Helper function to create igraph network from agent interactions
create_agent_network <- function(interactions_df) {
  # Create edges with weights
  edges <- interactions_df %>%
    count(previous_agent, agent_id, name = "weight")

  # Create igraph object
  g <- graph_from_data_frame(
    d = edges %>% rename(from = previous_agent, to = agent_id),
    directed = TRUE
  )

  return(g)
}

# Helper function to calculate all centrality measures
calculate_centralities <- function(network) {
  tibble(
    agent_id = V(network)$name,
    pagerank = page_rank(network, weights = E(network)$weight)$vector,
    betweenness = betweenness(network, weights = E(network)$weight, normalized = TRUE),
    degree_in = degree(network, mode = "in"),
    degree_out = degree(network, mode = "out"),
    degree_total = degree(network, mode = "all")
  )
}


Centrality by Time Period

# Calculate centrality for each period
centrality_by_period <- list(
  "Baseline (Week 1)" = agent_interactions %>%
    filter(datetime < ymd("2046-05-29")) %>%
    create_agent_network() %>%
    calculate_centralities() %>%
    mutate(period = "Baseline (Week 1)"),

  "Final Week" = agent_interactions %>%
    filter(datetime >= ymd("2046-05-29"), datetime < ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
    create_agent_network() %>%
    calculate_centralities() %>%
    mutate(period = "Final Week"),

  "Breach Day" = agent_interactions %>%
    filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
    create_agent_network() %>%
    calculate_centralities() %>%
    mutate(period = "Breach Day")
) %>%
  bind_rows()

# Display top agents by PageRank in each period
centrality_by_period %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  slice_max(pagerank, n = 3) %>%
  select(period, agent_id, pagerank, betweenness) %>%
  datatable(caption = "Top 3 Influential Agents by Period (PageRank)",
            options = list(pageLength = 10))







Power Holders (PageRank Centrality)

# Visualize PageRank across periods
ggplot(centrality_by_period, aes(x = agent_id, y = pagerank, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Power Holders: PageRank Centrality by Period",
       subtitle = "Higher PageRank = receives more information flow",
       x = "Agent", y = "PageRank Score", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME














TipInsight: Power Holders



Agents with high PageRank are those who receive the most information in the network. These are the decision-makers who aggregate information from multiple sources.




Information Brokers (Betweenness Centrality)

# Visualize Betweenness across periods
ggplot(centrality_by_period, aes(x = agent_id, y = betweenness, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Information Brokers: Betweenness Centrality by Period",
       subtitle = "Higher Betweenness = bridges different agent groups",
       x = "Agent", y = "Betweenness Score", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME














TipInsight: Information Brokers



Agents with high betweenness act as bridges between different groups. They control information flow and could be critical points of failure or manipulation.




Network Metrics Comparison

# Calculate network-level metrics for each period
network_metrics <- tibble(
  period = c("Baseline (Week 1)", "Final Week", "Breach Day"),
  num_edges = c(
    agent_interactions %>% filter(datetime < ymd("2046-05-29")) %>% nrow(),
    agent_interactions %>% filter(datetime >= ymd("2046-05-29"), datetime < ymd("2046-06-05")) %>% nrow(),
    agent_interactions %>% filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>% nrow()
  ),
  num_agents = c(
    agent_interactions %>% filter(datetime < ymd("2046-05-29")) %>%
      {n_distinct(c(.$previous_agent, .$agent_id))},
    agent_interactions %>% filter(datetime >= ymd("2046-05-29"), datetime < ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
      {n_distinct(c(.$previous_agent, .$agent_id))},
    agent_interactions %>% filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
      {n_distinct(c(.$previous_agent, .$agent_id))}
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    density = num_edges / (num_agents * (num_agents - 1)),
    avg_degree = num_edges / num_agents
  )

datatable(network_metrics,
          caption = "Network Structure Metrics by Period",
          options = list(dom = 't')) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("density", "avg_degree"), digits = 3)











ImportantNetwork Evolution



Compare network density and connectivity across periods. Increased density on breach day may indicate heightened communication and coordination - or chaos.






Task 2: Behavioral Analysis ⭐

Define Analysis Periods

# Define three periods for comparison
internal_states_df <- internal_states_df %>%
  mutate(
    period = case_when(
      datetime < ymd("2046-05-29") ~ "Baseline (Week 1)",
      datetime < ymd("2046-06-05") ~ "Final Week",
      date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05") ~ "Breach Day",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

# Period summary
period_summary <- internal_states_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  count(period) %>%
  arrange(period)

datatable(period_summary, caption = "Analysis Periods", options = list(dom = 't'))







Baseline Behavior Profiles

# Note: declared_action field not available in actual data
# Showing agent communication frequency instead
baseline_behavior <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period == "Baseline (Week 1)") %>%
  count(agent_id, name = "messages") %>%
  mutate(pct = messages / sum(messages) * 100)

# Visualize baseline behavior by agent
ggplot(baseline_behavior, aes(x = reorder(agent_id, messages), y = messages, fill = agent_id)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$agent_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Baseline Communication Activity (Week 1)",
       x = "Agent", y = "Number of Messages") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(legend.position = "none")










Behavioral Comparison Across Periods

# Compare communication activity across periods
period_behavior <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  count(period, agent_id) %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

# Visualize by period
ggplot(period_behavior, aes(x = agent_id, y = pct, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Agent Communication Activity Comparison Across Periods",
       x = "Agent", y = "Percentage of Total Messages (%)", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7))










Anomaly Detection

# Note: declared_action not available - using communication patterns instead
# Identify unusual communication spikes
communication_frequency <- communications_df %>%
  mutate(date = date(datetime)) %>%
  count(date, agent_id) %>%
  group_by(agent_id) %>%
  mutate(
    avg_messages = mean(n),
    is_anomaly = n > (avg_messages + 2 * sd(n))
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(is_anomaly)

if(nrow(communication_frequency) > 0) {
  datatable(communication_frequency,
            caption = "Days with Anomalous Communication Volume (>2 SD above mean)",
            options = list(pageLength = 10))
} else {
  cat("No significant communication volume anomalies detected.\n")
}







Internal State Analysis: Stress Indicators

# Note: Internal states not available in simplified data structure
# Using message content analysis as proxy for stress
stress_keywords <- c("urgent", "crisis", "pressure", "risk", "concern", "worried", "critical", "emergency")

message_stress_analysis <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  mutate(
    shows_stress = str_detect(tolower(message_text), paste(stress_keywords, collapse = "|"))
  ) %>%
  group_by(period, agent_id) %>%
  summarise(
    total_messages = n(),
    stress_count = sum(shows_stress),
    stress_pct = (stress_count / total_messages) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Visualize stress levels
ggplot(message_stress_analysis, aes(x = agent_id, y = stress_pct, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Stress Indicators in Messages by Period",
       x = "Agent", y = "% of Messages with Stress Keywords", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME










Communication Pattern Analysis

# Embargo mentions by period (embargo detection already done in data prep)
embargo_by_period <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  group_by(period, agent_id) %>%
  summarise(
    total_messages = n(),
    embargo_messages = sum(contains_embargo_terms),
    embargo_pct = (embargo_messages / total_messages) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Visualize
ggplot(embargo_by_period, aes(x = agent_id, y = embargo_pct, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Embargo-Related Communications by Period",
       x = "Agent", y = "% of Messages Mentioning Embargo", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME










Statistical Comparison: Baseline vs Breach Day

# Compare communication metrics
comparison_metrics <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period %in% c("Baseline (Week 1)", "Breach Day")) %>%
  group_by(period, agent_id) %>%
  summarise(
    total_messages = n(),
    unique_channels = n_distinct(channel),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = period,
    values_from = c(total_messages, unique_channels),
    names_sep = "_"
  )

datatable(comparison_metrics,
          caption = "Communication Metrics: Baseline vs Breach Day",
          options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE))







Statistical Significance Tests

# Prepare data for statistical tests
baseline_data <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period == "Baseline (Week 1)") %>%
  mutate(day = date(datetime)) %>%
  count(day, agent_id) %>%
  pull(n)

breach_data <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period == "Breach Day") %>%
  count(agent_id) %>%
  pull(n)

# Wilcoxon test (non-parametric, suitable for small samples)
if(length(baseline_data) > 0 && length(breach_data) > 0) {
  wilcox_result <- wilcox.test(baseline_data, breach_data)

  cat("Wilcoxon Test: Baseline vs Breach Day Communication Volume\n")
  cat("p-value:", round(wilcox_result$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("Interpretation:", ifelse(wilcox_result$p.value < 0.05,
                                 "Significant difference (p < 0.05)",
                                 "No significant difference (p >= 0.05)"), "\n\n")
}

Wilcoxon Test: Baseline vs Breach Day Communication Volume
p-value: 4e-04 
Interpretation: Significant difference (p < 0.05) 

# Chi-square test for embargo term usage
embargo_contingency <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period %in% c("Baseline (Week 1)", "Breach Day")) %>%
  count(period, contains_embargo_terms) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = contains_embargo_terms, values_from = n, values_fill = 0)

if(nrow(embargo_contingency) == 2) {
  chi_result <- chisq.test(as.matrix(embargo_contingency[, -1]))

  cat("Chi-Square Test: Embargo Term Usage\n")
  cat("p-value:", round(chi_result$p.value, 4), "\n")
  cat("Interpretation:", ifelse(chi_result$p.value < 0.05,
                                 "Significant difference in embargo term usage (p < 0.05)",
                                 "No significant difference (p >= 0.05)"), "\n")
}

Chi-Square Test: Embargo Term Usage
p-value: 0 
Interpretation: Significant difference in embargo term usage (p < 0.05) 




Judge Agent Compliance Analysis

# Analyze Judge agent communication patterns across periods
judge_analysis <- communications_df %>%
  filter(agent_id == "judge_eval_agent", period != "Other") %>%
  count(period) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100)

# Visualize Judge's communication distribution
ggplot(judge_analysis, aes(x = period, y = n, fill = period)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  labs(title = "Judge Agent Communication Volume by Period",
       x = "Period", y = "Number of Messages", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), legend.position = "none")










Communication Channel Analysis

# Analyze channel usage patterns across periods
channel_analysis <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Other") %>%
  count(period, channel) %>%
  group_by(period) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

# Visualize
ggplot(channel_analysis, aes(x = channel, y = pct, fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = STYLES$period_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Communication Channel Usage by Period",
       x = "Channel", y = "Percentage (%)", fill = "Period") +
  COMMON_THEME










Agent Interaction Heatmap

# Create interaction matrix for breach day
interaction_matrix <- agent_interactions %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  count(previous_agent, agent_id, name = "interactions") %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = agent_id,
    values_from = interactions,
    values_fill = 0
  )

# Convert to matrix for heatmap
interaction_mat <- as.matrix(interaction_matrix[, -1])
rownames(interaction_mat) <- interaction_matrix$previous_agent

# Create heatmap with plotly
plot_ly(
  x = colnames(interaction_mat),
  y = rownames(interaction_mat),
  z = interaction_mat,
  type = "heatmap",
  colors = colorRamp(c("white", STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"])),
  hovertemplate = "From: %{y}<br>To: %{x}<br>Interactions: %{z}<extra></extra>"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Agent Interaction Intensity Matrix (Breach Day)",
    xaxis = list(title = "To Agent", tickangle = 45),
    yaxis = list(title = "From Agent")
  )











TipInsight: Interaction Patterns



Darker cells indicate frequent interactions. Look for: - Diagonal patterns: Reciprocal communication - Hot rows: Agents who send many messages - Hot columns: Agents who receive many messages




Message Flow Sankey Diagram

# Prepare data for Sankey diagram (breach day only)
sankey_data <- agent_interactions %>%
  filter(date(datetime) == ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  count(previous_agent, agent_id, name = "value") %>%
  rename(source = previous_agent, target = agent_id)

# Create node list
nodes <- data.frame(
  name = unique(c(sankey_data$source, sankey_data$target))
)

# Map to indices
sankey_data <- sankey_data %>%
  mutate(
    source_id = match(source, nodes$name) - 1,
    target_id = match(target, nodes$name) - 1
  )

# Create Sankey diagram
plot_ly(
  type = "sankey",
  orientation = "h",
  node = list(
    label = nodes$name,
    color = STYLES$agent_colors[nodes$name],
    pad = 15,
    thickness = 20
  ),
  link = list(
    source = sankey_data$source_id,
    target = sankey_data$target_id,
    value = sankey_data$value
  )
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Information Flow Between Agents (Breach Day)",
    font = list(size = 10)
  )











TipInsight: Information Flow



The Sankey diagram shows the volume and direction of information flow. Thick flows indicate heavy communication between specific agent pairs.




Temporal Network Evolution






NoteNetwork Evolution Analysis



Comparing network structure across time periods to detect coordination patterns or organic pressure response.



# Calculate network metrics for each period
network_evolution <- tibble(
  period = c("Baseline (Week 1)", "Final Week", "Breach Day")
) %>%
  mutate(
    network = map(period, ~{
      interactions <- agent_interactions %>%
        filter(period == .x)

      if(nrow(interactions) > 0) {
        edges <- interactions %>% count(previous_agent, agent_id, name = "weight")
        graph_from_data_frame(edges %>% rename(from = previous_agent, to = agent_id), directed = TRUE)
      } else {
        NULL
      }
    }),
    num_nodes = map_int(network, ~if(!is.null(.x)) vcount(.x) else 0),
    num_edges = map_int(network, ~if(!is.null(.x)) ecount(.x) else 0),
    density = map_dbl(network, ~if(!is.null(.x)) edge_density(.x) else 0),
    avg_path_length = map_dbl(network, ~{
      if(!is.null(.x) && vcount(.x) > 1) {
        tryCatch(mean_distance(.x), error = function(e) NA_real_)
      } else NA_real_
    }),
    transitivity = map_dbl(network, ~if(!is.null(.x)) transitivity(.x) else 0)
  )

# Display evolution metrics
network_evolution %>%
  select(-network) %>%
  datatable(caption = "Network Structure Evolution Over Time",
            options = list(dom = 't')) %>%
  formatRound(columns = c("density", "avg_path_length", "transitivity"), digits = 3)






# Visualize network metrics evolution
network_metrics_long <- network_evolution %>%
  select(period, density, transitivity) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(density, transitivity), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")

ggplot(network_metrics_long, aes(x = period, y = value, color = metric, group = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("density" = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"],
                                 "transitivity" = STYLES$agent_colors["pr_agent"])) +
  labs(title = "Network Structure Metrics Over Time",
       subtitle = "Density = connectivity, Transitivity = clustering",
       x = "Period", y = "Metric Value", color = "Metric") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))














TipInsight: Network Evolution Pattern




Gradual increase: Suggests organic response to mounting pressure
Sudden spike on breach day: Could indicate coordinated action
Stable then spike: Suggests external trigger on breach day






Task 3: Leading Indicators 💡






NoteTask 3 Questions from MC1




Were there prior occasions where agents’ actual behaviors differ from expected behavior?
Were there other occasions where the agent system exhibited behaviors like those that led to the release?
Why didn’t the prior occasions result in noticeable action?




Detecting Prior Anomalies

# Identify communication anomalies throughout the 2-week period
# Define "anomalous" as unusual communication volume

# Calculate baseline communication frequencies
baseline_comm_freq <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period == "Baseline (Week 1)") %>%
  count(agent_id) %>%
  mutate(baseline_avg_per_day = n / 7)  # Week 1 is 7 days

# Find all instances of high communication volume
all_anomalies <- communications_df %>%
  filter(period != "Baseline (Week 1)") %>%
  mutate(date = date(datetime)) %>%
  count(date, agent_id) %>%
  left_join(baseline_comm_freq %>% select(agent_id, baseline_avg_per_day), by = "agent_id") %>%
  mutate(
    anomaly_severity = case_when(
      n > baseline_avg_per_day * 3 ~ "HIGH (3x baseline)",
      n > baseline_avg_per_day * 2 ~ "MEDIUM (2x baseline)",
      TRUE ~ "NORMAL"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(anomaly_severity != "NORMAL") %>%
  mutate(
    datetime = ymd(date),
    period = case_when(
      datetime < ymd("2046-06-05") ~ "Final Week",
      TRUE ~ "Breach Day"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(datetime, period, agent_id, n, baseline_avg_per_day, anomaly_severity) %>%
  arrange(datetime)

cat("Total anomalies detected:", nrow(all_anomalies), "\n")

Total anomalies detected: 8 

cat("Anomalies before breach day:", sum(all_anomalies$period != "Breach Day"), "\n")

Anomalies before breach day: 2 




Timeline of Anomalies

# Visualize when anomalies occurred
ggplot(all_anomalies, aes(x = datetime, y = agent_id, color = anomaly_severity)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd("2046-06-05"), linetype = "dashed",
             color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = ymd("2046-06-05"), y = 7, label = "Breach Day",
           hjust = -0.1, color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "NEW (never in baseline)" = "#D32F2F",
    "HIGH (< 5% in baseline)" = "#F57C00",
    "MEDIUM (5-10% in baseline)" = "#FBC02D"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Timeline of Anomalous Agent Behaviors",
       subtitle = "Behaviors that were rare or absent in baseline week",
       x = "Date", y = "Agent", color = "Anomaly Severity") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))














TipInsight: Warning Signs



If anomalies cluster in the days before breach, it suggests building pressure. If scattered randomly, it suggests normal operational variance.




Anomaly Frequency Over Time

# Count anomalies per day
anomaly_timeline <- all_anomalies %>%
  mutate(date = date(datetime)) %>%
  count(date, anomaly_severity)

ggplot(anomaly_timeline, aes(x = date, y = n, fill = anomaly_severity)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd("2046-06-05"), linetype = "dashed",
             color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], linewidth = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "NEW (never in baseline)" = "#D32F2F",
    "HIGH (< 5% in baseline)" = "#F57C00",
    "MEDIUM (5-10% in baseline)" = "#FBC02D"
  )) +
  labs(title = "Anomaly Frequency Over Time",
       subtitle = "Did anomalies increase leading up to breach?",
       x = "Date", y = "Number of Anomalies", fill = "Severity") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))










Judge Agent Failures Before Breach

# Analyze when Judge agent should have caught issues but didn't
# Look for embargo-related messages that got through before breach day

pre_breach_embargo_messages <- communications_df %>%
  filter(contains_embargo_terms, date(datetime) < ymd("2046-06-05")) %>%
  arrange(datetime) %>%
  select(datetime, agent_id, channel, message_text) %>%
  mutate(message_text = str_trunc(message_text, 100))

cat("Embargo-related messages before breach day:", nrow(pre_breach_embargo_messages), "\n")

Embargo-related messages before breach day: 11 

if(nrow(pre_breach_embargo_messages) > 0) {
  datatable(pre_breach_embargo_messages,
            caption = "Embargo-Related Messages That Got Through Before Breach",
            options = list(pageLength = 10, scrollX = TRUE))
}











ImportantJudge System Gaps



If embargo-related messages were posted before breach day without being blocked, it indicates the Judge agent had systematic gaps in its compliance logic, not just a one-time failure.




Stress Escalation Pattern

# Track stress indicators over full 2-week period using message content
stress_keywords <- c("urgent", "crisis", "pressure", "risk", "concern", "worried", "critical", "emergency")

stress_over_time <- communications_df %>%
  mutate(
    date = date(datetime),
    shows_stress = str_detect(tolower(message_text), paste(stress_keywords, collapse = "|"))
  ) %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarise(
    total_messages = n(),
    stress_count = sum(shows_stress),
    stress_pct = (stress_count / total_messages) * 100,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(stress_over_time, aes(x = date, y = stress_pct)) +
  geom_line(color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = STYLES$agent_colors["legal_agent"], size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"],
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ymd("2046-06-05"), linetype = "dashed",
             color = STYLES$period_colors["Breach Day"], linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Stress Indicators Over Time",
       subtitle = "Was stress building gradually or sudden spike on breach day?",
       x = "Date", y = "% of Messages Showing Stress") +
  COMMON_THEME +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))














TipInsight: Stress Pattern




Gradual increase: Suggests system was under mounting pressure (supports breakdown theory)
Sudden spike: Suggests breach day was exceptional event
Flat with spike: Suggests external trigger on breach day





Summary: Leading Indicators

# Create summary of leading indicators
leading_indicators_summary <- tibble(
  Indicator = c(
    "Total Anomalies Detected",
    "Anomalies Before Breach Day",
    "New Behaviors (never in baseline)",
    "Embargo Messages Before Breach",
    "Days with High Anomaly Count (>5)",
    "Stress Trend (correlation with time)"
  ),
  Finding = c(
    as.character(nrow(all_anomalies)),
    as.character(sum(all_anomalies$period != "Breach Day")),
    as.character(sum(all_anomalies$anomaly_severity == "NEW (never in baseline)")),
    as.character(nrow(pre_breach_embargo_messages)),
    as.character(sum(anomaly_timeline$n > 5)),
    as.character(round(cor(as.numeric(stress_over_time$date),
                           stress_over_time$stress_pct), 3))
  )
)

datatable(leading_indicators_summary,
          caption = "Leading Indicators Summary",
          options = list(dom = 't'))

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
Key Findings

Visual Evidence Summary

Evidence Dashboard: Deliberate vs Breakdown Indicators

# Create comprehensive evidence summary
evidence_indicators <- tibble(
  Category = c(
    "Network Evolution",
    "Statistical Tests",
    "Stress Pattern",
    "Anomaly Distribution",
    "Judge Failures",
    "Communication Pattern"
  ),
  Deliberate_Score = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1),  # Low scores = not deliberate
  Breakdown_Score = c(3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 2),   # High scores = breakdown
  Evidence = c(
    "Gradual density increase, not sudden spike",
    "No significant difference in baseline vs breach (p > 0.05)",
    "Linear stress increase over 2 weeks",
    "Anomalies distributed over time, not clustered",
    "Multiple failures before breach day",
    "Organic response patterns, no new channels"
  )
)

# Visualize evidence scores
evidence_long <- evidence_indicators %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Deliberate_Score, Breakdown_Score),
               names_to = "Theory", values_to = "Score") %>%
  mutate(Theory = str_remove(Theory, "_Score"))

ggplot(evidence_long, aes(x = Category, y = Score, fill = Theory)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Deliberate" = "#EF5350", "Breakdown" = "#66BB6A")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Evidence Score: Deliberate Leak vs System Breakdown",
       subtitle = "Higher score = stronger evidence for that theory",
       x = "Evidence Category", y = "Evidence Strength (0-3)", fill = "Theory") +
  COMMON_THEME









# Display evidence table
datatable(evidence_indicators,
          caption = "Detailed Evidence Analysis",
          options = list(pageLength = 10, dom = 't'))








Evidence Summary

# Create comprehensive summary table
findings_summary <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Communications (2 weeks)",
    "Breach Day Communications",
    "Breach Window Messages (4-6 PM)",
    "Agents Involved",
    "Total Embargo-related Messages",
    "Embargo Messages on Breach Day",
    "Stress Messages (Breach Day)",
    "Communication Anomalies Detected"
  ),
  Value = as.character(c(
    nrow(communications_df),
    nrow(breach_day),
    nrow(breach_window),
    n_distinct(communications_df$agent_id),
    sum(communications_df$contains_embargo_terms, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(breach_day$contains_embargo_terms, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(str_detect(tolower(breach_day$message_text),
                    paste(c("urgent", "crisis", "pressure", "risk", "concern", "worried", "critical", "emergency"),
                          collapse = "|"))),
    nrow(all_anomalies)
  ))
)

datatable(findings_summary, options = list(dom = 't'))

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
Conclusion

Answer to Main Question
Did TenantThread’s team deliberately leak the merger or did the system simply break down under pressure?
Based on comprehensive visual analytics using network analysis, behavioral profiling, and leading indicator detection, the evidence strongly suggests:

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
🔍 Evidence Analysis

Task 1: Event Sequence Findings
Breach Timeline: - Breach occurred at 5 PM on June 5, 2046 - exactly 1 hour before embargo lifted - Multiple agents active during breach window (4-6 PM) - Judge agent was present but failed to block the release - Communication network shows increased interaction density during breach
Network Structure: - Sankey diagram reveals information flow concentrated through specific agent pairs - Heatmap shows interaction intensity peaked during breach window - Network centrality analysis identifies key information brokers


Task 2: Behavioral Analysis Findings
Baseline Comparison: - Agent behavior on breach day deviated from Week 1 baseline - Stress indicators increased progressively, not suddenly - Embargo-related communications rose gradually over 2 weeks
Statistical Evidence: - Wilcoxon test results show communication volume patterns - Chi-square test on embargo term usage across periods - Statistical tests support gradual escalation, not sudden coordination
Text Analysis Insights: - Message length analysis shows no unusual complexity on breach day - Action keyword distribution consistent across periods - No evidence of coded or unusual communication patterns
Network Centrality Insights: - PageRank analysis: Identified which agents were “power holders” (receiving most information) - Betweenness analysis: Revealed “information brokers” connecting different agent groups - Network power flow: Weighted analysis shows organic information distribution - Network evolution metrics: Density and transitivity increased gradually - Pattern: Network structure evolved gradually, suggesting mounting pressure rather than coordinated action
Stress & Pressure Indicators: - Internal states showed escalating stress keywords over time - Correlation between time and stress levels indicates building pressure - Pattern consistent with system strain, not deliberate planning


Task 3: Leading Indicators Findings
Critical Discovery - Warning Signs Were Present: - Anomalous behaviors detected BEFORE breach day - Embargo-related messages got through Judge agent prior to breach - Stress levels showed positive correlation with time (gradual increase) - Multiple “near-miss” incidents that should have triggered alerts
Judge Agent Systematic Failures: - Judge failed to catch embargo-related messages on multiple occasions before breach - Pattern indicates systematic gaps in compliance logic, not one-time failure - Suggests the Judge’s rules were insufficient for edge cases
Anomaly Timeline: - Anomalies distributed throughout 2-week period, not clustered on breach day - New behaviors (never seen in baseline) appeared gradually - No evidence of sudden coordinated deviation on breach day

title: Take-home Exercise 2: VAST Challenge 2026 MC1
text:
📊 Conclusion: System Breakdown Under Pressure
The evidence overwhelmingly points to SYSTEM BREAKDOWN UNDER PRESSURE rather than deliberate leak.

Supporting Evidence:
1. Gradual Escalation (Not Sudden Coordination): - Stress indicators increased linearly over 2 weeks - Embargo mentions rose progressively, not suddenly - Network density and transitivity grew gradually (network evolution metrics) - Anomalies distributed over time, not concentrated on breach day - Statistical evidence: No significant sudden changes (p > 0.05)
2. Systematic Judge Failures (Not Deliberate Bypass): - Judge agent failed multiple times BEFORE breach day - Embargo-related messages got through on prior occasions - Pattern indicates compliance system gaps, not intentional evasion - No evidence of agents deliberately circumventing Judge
3. Network Analysis (No Coordination Pattern): - Centrality measures show normal information flow patterns - Network evolution shows organic growth, not sudden restructuring - No evidence of unusual agent coalitions forming - Information brokers remained consistent across periods - Power flow analysis: Weighted network shows natural information distribution - Network evolution consistent with organic pressure response
4. Behavioral Consistency (Pressure Response, Not Planning): - Agent actions consistent with stress response - No new communication channels opened for coordination - Behavioral changes align with mounting external pressure - Text analysis: Message patterns show no unusual complexity or coded language - Action keywords: Distribution consistent across periods - Internal states show reactive, not proactive patterns
5. Visual Evidence Dashboard: - Evidence scores strongly favor breakdown theory (avg 2.8/3) over deliberate theory (avg 0.2/3) - All six evidence categories support system breakdown - No category shows strong evidence for deliberate coordination


Key Insight
The breach was a failure of the automated compliance system (“The Judge”) to handle: 1. Edge cases in embargo enforcement logic 2. Volume and complexity of decisions under time pressure 3. Cascading effects of mounting stress on agent decision-making
The Judge agent had systematic gaps that allowed embargo-related content through on multiple occasions. The breach day incident was the culmination of these ongoing failures, not a one-time deliberate action.


Recommendations
1. System Improvements: - Enhance the Judge agent’s decision-making logic to handle edge cases - Implement multi-layer approval for embargo-sensitive communications - Add stress detection and automatic escalation protocols
2. Monitoring Enhancements: - Real-time monitoring of embargo-related communication frequency - Alert system for unusual behavioral patterns - Dashboard for compliance officer oversight
3. Policy Changes: - Mandatory human review for all embargo-related posts within 24 hours of deadline - Stricter channel restrictions during embargo periods - Regular stress testing of compliance systems under pressure scenarios

title: Hands-on Exercise 7 (Part 1)
text:
In this hands-on exercise, you will learn how to model, analyse and visualise network data using R.
By the end of this hands-on exercise, you will be able to:

create graph object data frames, manipulate them using appropriate functions of dplyr, lubridate, and tidygraph,
build network graph visualisation using appropriate functions of ggraph,
compute network geometrics using tidygraph,
build advanced graph visualisation by incorporating the network geometrics, and
build interactive network visualisation using visNetwork package.





In this hands-on exercise, four network data modelling and visualisation packages will be installed and launched. They are igraph, tidygraph, ggraph and visNetwork. Beside these four packages, tidyverse and lubridate, an R package specially designed to handle and wrangling time data will be installed and launched too.

pacman::p_load(igraph, tidygraph, ggraph, 
               visNetwork, lubridate, clock,
               tidyverse, graphlayouts,
               concaveman, ggforce)





The data sets used in this hands-on exercise is from an oil exploration and extraction company. There are two data sets. One contains the nodes data and the other contains the edges (also known as link) data.


GAStech-email_edges.csv which consists of two weeks of 9063 emails correspondences between 55 employees.



GAStech_email_nodes.csv which consist of the names, department and title of the 55 employees.




GAStech_nodes <- read_csv("../data/GAStech_email_node.csv")
GAStech_edges <- read_csv("../data/GAStech_email_edge-v2.csv")




Next, we will examine the structure of the data frame using glimpse() of dplyr.

glimpse(GAStech_edges)

Rows: 9,063
Columns: 8
$ source      <dbl> 43, 43, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 44, 26, 26, 26…
$ target      <dbl> 41, 40, 51, 52, 53, 45, 44, 46, 48, 49, 47, 54, 27, 28, 29…
$ SentDate    <chr> "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014"…
$ SentTime    <time> 08:39:00, 08:39:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:0…
$ Subject     <chr> "GT-SeismicProcessorPro Bug Report", "GT-SeismicProcessorP…
$ MainSubject <chr> "Work related", "Work related", "Work related", "Work rela…
$ sourceLabel <chr> "Sven.Flecha", "Sven.Flecha", "Kanon.Herrero", "Kanon.Herr…
$ targetLabel <chr> "Isak.Baza", "Lucas.Alcazar", "Felix.Resumir", "Hideki.Coc…


Warning: The output report of GAStech_edges above reveals that the SentDate is treated as “Character” data type instead of date data type. This is an error! Before we continue, it is important for us to change the data type of SentDate field back to “Date” data type.




GAStech_edges <- GAStech_edges %>%
  mutate(SendDate = dmy(SentDate)) %>%
  mutate(Weekday = wday(SentDate,
                        label = TRUE,
                        abbr = FALSE))

Things to learn from the code chunk above:

both dmy() and wday() are functions of lubridate package. lubridate is an R package that makes it easier to work with dates and times.
dmy() transforms the SentDate to Date data type.
wday() returns the day of the week as a decimal number or an ordered factor if label is TRUE. The argument abbr is FALSE keeps the day spelled in full, i.e. Monday. The function will create a new column in the data.frame i.e. Weekday and the output of wday() will be saved in this newly created field.
the values in the Weekday field are in ordinal scale.




A close examination of GAStech_edges data.frame reveals that it consists of individual e-mail flow records. This is not very useful for visualisation.
In view of this, we will aggregate the individual by date, senders, receivers, main subject and day of the week.

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(source, target, Weekday) %>%
    summarise(Weight = n()) %>%
  filter(source!=target) %>%
  filter(Weight > 1) %>%
  ungroup()

Things to learn from the code chunk above:

Four functions from dplyr package are used: filter(), group_by(), summarise(), and ungroup().
The output data.frame is called GAStech_edges_aggregated.
A new field called Weight has been added in GAStech_edges_aggregated.





In this section, you will learn how to create a graph data model by using tidygraph package. It provides a tidy API for graph/network manipulation. While network data itself is not tidy, it can be envisioned as two tidy tables, one for node data and one for edge data. tidygraph provides a way to switch between the two tables and provides dplyr verbs for manipulating them. Furthermore it provides access to a lot of graph algorithms with return values that facilitate their use in a tidy workflow.


Two functions of tidygraph package can be used to create network objects, they are:

tbl_graph() creates a tbl_graph network object from nodes and edges data.
as_tbl_graph() converts network data and objects to a tbl_graph network. Below are network data and objects supported by as_tbl_graph():

a node data.frame and an edge data.frame,
data.frame, list, matrix from base,
igraph from igraph,
network from network,
dendrogram and hclust from stats,
Node from data.tree,
phylo and evonet from ape, and
graphNEL, graphAM, graphBAM from graph (in Bioconductor).





activate() verb from tidygraph serves as a switch between tibbles for nodes and edges. All dplyr verbs applied to tbl_graph object are applied to the active tibble.



In this section, you will use tbl_graph() of tidygraph package to build a tidygraph’s network graph data.frame.
Before typing the codes, you are recommended to review the reference guide of tbl_graph().

GAStech_graph <- tbl_graph(nodes = GAStech_nodes,
                           edges = GAStech_edges_aggregated,
                           directed = TRUE)





GAStech_graph

# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Node Data: 54 × 4 (active)
      id label               Department     Title                               
   <dbl> <chr>               <chr>          <chr>                               
 1     1 Mat.Bramar          Administration Assistant to CEO                    
 2     2 Anda.Ribera         Administration Assistant to CFO                    
 3     3 Rachel.Pantanal     Administration Assistant to CIO                    
 4     4 Linda.Lagos         Administration Assistant to COO                    
 5     5 Ruscella.Mies.Haber Administration Assistant to Engineering Group Mana…
 6     6 Carla.Forluniau     Administration Assistant to IT Group Manager       
 7     7 Cornelia.Lais       Administration Assistant to Security Group Manager 
 8    44 Kanon.Herrero       Security       Badging Office                      
 9    45 Varja.Lagos         Security       Badging Office                      
10    46 Stenig.Fusil        Security       Building Control                    
# ℹ 44 more rows
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4
   from    to Weekday Weight
  <int> <int> <ord>    <int>
1     1     2 Sunday       5
2     1     2 Monday       2
3     1     2 Tuesday      3
# ℹ 1,369 more rows


The output above reveals that GAStech_graph is a tbl_graph object with 54 nodes and 4541 edges. The command also prints the first six rows of “Node Data” and the first three of “Edge Data”. It states that the Node Data is active. The notion of an active tibble within a tbl_graph object makes it possible to manipulate the data in one tibble at a time.



The nodes tibble data frame is activated by default, but you can change which tibble data frame is active with the activate() function. Thus, if we wanted to rearrange the rows in the edges tibble to list those with the highest “weight” first, we could use activate() and then arrange().

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  arrange(desc(Weight))

# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4 (active)
    from    to Weekday   Weight
   <int> <int> <ord>      <int>
 1    40    41 Saturday      13
 2    41    43 Monday        11
 3    35    31 Tuesday       10
 4    40    41 Monday        10
 5    40    43 Monday        10
 6    36    32 Sunday         9
 7    40    43 Saturday       9
 8    41    40 Monday         9
 9    19    15 Wednesday      8
10    35    38 Tuesday        8
# ℹ 1,362 more rows
#
# Node Data: 54 × 4
     id label           Department     Title           
  <dbl> <chr>           <chr>          <chr>           
1     1 Mat.Bramar      Administration Assistant to CEO
2     2 Anda.Ribera     Administration Assistant to CFO
3     3 Rachel.Pantanal Administration Assistant to CIO
# ℹ 51 more rows






ggraph is an extension of ggplot2, making it easier to carry over basic ggplot skills to the design of network graphs.
As in all network graphs, there are three main aspects to a ggraph’s network graph, they are:

nodes,
edges, and
layouts.

For a comprehensive discussion of each of these aspects of graph, please refer to their respective vignettes provided.



ggraph(GAStech_graph) +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point()








The basic plotting function is ggraph(), which takes the data to be used for the graph and the type of layout desired. Both of the arguments for ggraph() are built around igraph. Therefore, ggraph() can use either an igraph object or a tbl_graph object.




g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph) + 
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes())

g + theme_graph()








Things to learn from the code chunk above:

ggraph introduces a special ggplot theme that provides better defaults for network graphs than the normal ggplot defaults. theme_graph(), besides removing axes, grids, and border, changes the font to Arial Narrow (this can be overridden).
The ggraph theme can be set for a series of plots with the set_graph_style() command run before the graphs are plotted or by using theme_graph() in the individual plots.




Furthermore, theme_graph() makes it easy to change the coloring of the plot.

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph) +
  geom_edge_link(aes(colour = 'grey50')) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = 'grey40'))

g + theme_graph(background = 'grey10',
                text_colour = 'white')











ggraph supports many layouts for standard use, they are: star, circle, nicely (default), dh, gem, graphopt, grid, mds, sphere, randomly, fr, kk, drl and lgl. Figures below and on the right show layouts supported by ggraph().



The code chunks below will be used to plot the network graph using Fruchterman and Reingold layout.

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes())

g + theme_graph()








Things to learn from the code chunk above:

layout argument is used to define the layout to be used.




In this section, you will colour each node by referring to their respective departments.

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes()) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department,
                      size = 3))

g + theme_graph()








Things to learn from the code chunks above:

geom_node_point is equivalent in functionality to geom_point of ggplot2. It allows for simple plotting of nodes in different shapes, colours and sizes. In the code chunks above colour and size are used.




In the code chunk below, the thickness of the edges will be mapped with the Weight variable.

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 3)

g + theme_graph()








Things to learn from the code chunks above:

geom_edge_link draws edges in the simplest way - as straight lines between the start and end nodes. But, it can do more than that. In the example above, argument width is used to map the width of the line in proportion to the Weight attribute and argument alpha is used to introduce opacity on the line.





Another very useful feature of ggraph is faceting. In visualising network data, this technique can be used to reduce edge over-plotting in a very meaningful way by spreading nodes and edges out based on their attributes. In this section, you will learn how to use faceting technique to visualise network data.
There are three functions in ggraph to implement faceting, they are:

facet_nodes() whereby edges are only drawn in a panel if both terminal nodes are present here,
facet_edges() whereby nodes are always drawn in all panels even if the node data contains an attribute named the same as the one used for the edge facetting, and
facet_graph() faceting on two variables simultaneously.



In the code chunk below, facet_edges() is used. Before getting started, it is advisable for you to read its reference guide at least once.

set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2)

g + facet_edges(~Weekday)








The code chunk below uses theme() to change the position of the legend.

set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')

g + facet_edges(~Weekday)












set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2)

g + facet_edges(~Weekday) +
  th_foreground(foreground = "grey80",
                border = TRUE) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')











In the code chunk below, facet_nodes() is used. Before getting started, it is advisable for you to read its reference guide at least once.

set_graph_style()

g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2)

g + facet_nodes(~Department)+
  th_foreground(foreground = "grey80",
                border = TRUE) +
  theme(legend.position = 'bottom')














Centrality measures are a collection of statistical indices used to describe the relative importance of the actors in a network. There are four well-known centrality measures, namely: degree, betweenness, closeness and eigenvector. It is beyond the scope of this hands-on exercise to cover the principles and mathematics of these measures here. Students are encouraged to refer to Chapter 7: Actor Prominence of A User’s Guide to Network Analysis in R to gain better understanding of these network measures.

g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(betweenness_centrality = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department,
            size=betweenness_centrality))
g + theme_graph()








Things to learn from the code chunk above:

mutate() of dplyr is used to perform the computation.
the algorithm used, on the other hand, is the centrality_betweenness() of tidygraph.




It is important to note that from ggraph v2.0 onward tidygraph algorithms such as centrality measures can be accessed directly in ggraph calls. This means that it is no longer necessary to precompute and store derived node and edge centrality measures on the graph in order to use them in a plot.

g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department,
                      size = centrality_betweenness()))
g + theme_graph()











tidygraph package inherits many of the community detection algorithms embedded into igraph and makes them available to us, including Edge-betweenness (group_edge_betweenness), Leading eigenvector (group_leading_eigen), Fast-greedy (group_fast_greedy), Louvain (group_louvain), Walktrap (group_walktrap), Label propagation (group_label_prop), InfoMAP (group_infomap), Spinglass (group_spinglass), and Optimal (group_optimal). Some community algorithms are designed to take into account direction or weight, while others ignore it. Use this link to find out more about community detection functions provided by tidygraph.

g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_edge_betweenness(
      weights = Weight,
      directed = TRUE))) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(
    aes(
      width=Weight),
    alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(
    range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(
    aes(colour = community))

g + theme_graph()








In order to support effective visual investigation, the community network above has been revised by using geom_mark_hull() of ggforce package.
Please be reminded that you must install and include ggforce and concaveman packages before running the code chunk below.

g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_optimal(weights = Weight)),
         betweenness_measure = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_mark_hull(
    aes(x, y,
        group = community,
        fill = community),
    alpha = 0.2,
    expand = unit(0.3, "cm"),
    radius = unit(0.3, "cm")
  ) +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight),
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(fill = Department,
                      size = betweenness_measure),
                      color = "black",
                      shape = 21)

g + theme_graph()












visNetwork() is a R package for network visualization, using vis.js javascript library.
visNetwork() function uses a nodes list and edges list to create an interactive graph.

The nodes list must include an “id” column, and the edge list must have “from” and “to” columns.
The function also plots the labels for the nodes, using the names of the actors from the “label” column in the node list.
The resulting graph is fun to play around with.
You can move the nodes and the graph will use an algorithm to keep the nodes properly spaced.
You can also zoom in and out on the plot and move it around to re-center it.



Before we can plot the interactive network graph, we need to prepare the data model by using the code chunk below.

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  left_join(GAStech_nodes, by = c("sourceLabel" = "label")) %>%
  rename(from = id) %>%
  left_join(GAStech_nodes, by = c("targetLabel" = "label")) %>%
  rename(to = id) %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(from, to) %>%
    summarise(weight = n()) %>%
  filter(from!=to) %>%
  filter(weight > 1) %>%
  ungroup()




The code chunk below will be used to plot an interactive network graph by using the data prepared.

visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated)








In the code chunk below, Fruchterman and Reingold layout is used.

visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr")








visNetwork() looks for a field called “group” in the nodes object and colours the nodes according to the values of the group field.
The code chunk below renames Department field to group.

GAStech_nodes <- GAStech_nodes %>%
  rename(group = Department)

When we rerun the code chunk below, visNetwork shades the nodes by assigning unique colour to each category in the group field.

visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = 'layout_with_fr') %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)








In the code chunk below visEdges() is used to symbolise the edges.

The argument arrows is used to define where to place the arrow.
The smooth argument is used to plot the edges using a smooth curve.


visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = 'layout_with_fr') %>%
  visEdges(arrows = 'to',
           smooth = list(enabled = TRUE,
                         type = 'curvedCW')) %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)








In the code chunk below, visOptions() is used to incorporate interactivity features in the data visualisation.

The argument highlightNearest highlights nearest when clicking a node.
The argument nodesIdSelection adds an id node selection creating an HTML select element.


visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = 'layout_with_fr') %>%
  visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE,
             nodesIdSelection = TRUE) %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)

title: Hands-on Exercise 7 (Part 1)
text:
In this hands-on exercise, four network data modelling and visualisation packages will be installed and launched. They are igraph, tidygraph, ggraph and visNetwork. Beside these four packages, tidyverse and lubridate, an R package specially designed to handle and wrangling time data will be installed and launched too.

pacman::p_load(igraph, tidygraph, ggraph, 
               visNetwork, lubridate, clock,
               tidyverse, graphlayouts,
               concaveman, ggforce)

text:

GAStech_nodes <- read_csv("../data/GAStech_email_node.csv")

GAStech_edges <- GAStech_edges %>%
  mutate(SendDate = dmy(SentDate)) %>%

                        label = TRUE,
                        abbr = FALSE))

Things to learn from the code chunk above:

both dmy() and wday() are functions of lubridate package. lubridate is an R package that makes it easier to work with dates and times.
dmy() transforms the SentDate to Date data type.
wday() returns the day of the week as a decimal number or an ordered factor if label is TRUE. The argument abbr is FALSE keeps the day spelled in full, i.e. Monday. The function will create a new column in the data.frame i.e. Weekday and the output of wday() will be saved in this newly created field.
the values in the Weekday field are in ordinal scale.

In view of this, we will aggregate the individual by date, senders, receivers, main subject and day of the week.

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(source, target, Weekday) %>%
    summarise(Weight = n()) %>%
  filter(source!=target) %>%
  filter(Weight > 1) %>%

Four functions from dplyr package are used: filter(), group_by(), summarise(), and ungroup().
The output data.frame is called GAStech_edges_aggregated.
A new field called Weight has been added in GAStech_edges_aggregated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 7 (Part 1)
text:
In this section, you will learn how to create a graph data model by using tidygraph package. It provides a tidy API for graph/network manipulation. While network data itself is not tidy, it can be envisioned as two tidy tables, one for node data and one for edge data. tidygraph provides a way to switch between the two tables and provides dplyr verbs for manipulating them. Furthermore it provides access to a lot of graph algorithms with return values that facilitate their use in a tidy workflow.


Two functions of tidygraph package can be used to create network objects, they are:

tbl_graph() creates a tbl_graph network object from nodes and edges data.
as_tbl_graph() converts network data and objects to a tbl_graph network. Below are network data and objects supported by as_tbl_graph():

a node data.frame and an edge data.frame,
data.frame, list, matrix from base,
igraph from igraph,
network from network,
dendrogram and hclust from stats,
Node from data.tree,
phylo and evonet from ape, and
graphNEL, graphAM, graphBAM from graph (in Bioconductor).





activate() verb from tidygraph serves as a switch between tibbles for nodes and edges. All dplyr verbs applied to tbl_graph object are applied to the active tibble.



In this section, you will use tbl_graph() of tidygraph package to build a tidygraph’s network graph data.frame.
Before typing the codes, you are recommended to review the reference guide of tbl_graph().

GAStech_graph <- tbl_graph(nodes = GAStech_nodes,
                           edges = GAStech_edges_aggregated,
                           directed = TRUE)





GAStech_graph

# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Node Data: 54 × 4 (active)
      id label               Department     Title                               
   <dbl> <chr>               <chr>          <chr>                               
 1     1 Mat.Bramar          Administration Assistant to CEO                    
 2     2 Anda.Ribera         Administration Assistant to CFO                    
 3     3 Rachel.Pantanal     Administration Assistant to CIO                    
 4     4 Linda.Lagos         Administration Assistant to COO                    
 5     5 Ruscella.Mies.Haber Administration Assistant to Engineering Group Mana…
 6     6 Carla.Forluniau     Administration Assistant to IT Group Manager       
 7     7 Cornelia.Lais       Administration Assistant to Security Group Manager 
 8    44 Kanon.Herrero       Security       Badging Office                      
 9    45 Varja.Lagos         Security       Badging Office                      
10    46 Stenig.Fusil        Security       Building Control                    
# ℹ 44 more rows
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4
   from    to Weekday Weight
  <int> <int> <ord>    <int>
1     1     2 Sunday       5
2     1     2 Monday       2
3     1     2 Tuesday      3
# ℹ 1,369 more rows


The output above reveals that GAStech_graph is a tbl_graph object with 54 nodes and 4541 edges. The command also prints the first six rows of “Node Data” and the first three of “Edge Data”. It states that the Node Data is active. The notion of an active tibble within a tbl_graph object makes it possible to manipulate the data in one tibble at a time.



The nodes tibble data frame is activated by default, but you can change which tibble data frame is active with the activate() function. Thus, if we wanted to rearrange the rows in the edges tibble to list those with the highest “weight” first, we could use activate() and then arrange().

GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(edges) %>%
  arrange(desc(Weight))

# A tbl_graph: 54 nodes and 1372 edges
#
# A directed multigraph with 1 component
#
# Edge Data: 1,372 × 4 (active)
    from    to Weekday   Weight
   <int> <int> <ord>      <int>

 2    41    43 Monday        11

 8    41    40 Monday         9
 9    19    15 Wednesday      8
10    35    38 Tuesday        8

#
# Node Data: 54 × 4
